skill.name: typescript-node-expert
skill.description: Guides TypeScript and Node.js development with strict typing, maintainable architecture, performance patterns, CLI structure, testing standards, and code review practices.
skill.summary: Apply strict, modern TypeScript and Node.js practices to application, CLI, and library work.
skill.author: DammianMiller

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and ordinary TypeScript guidance rather than executable skill behavior. No malicious intent, credential access, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden automation was found in SKILL.md. Residual risk is low because the skill teaches examples that include fetch, file streams, process.exit, and npm test or lint commands.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown inline code, fenced TypeScript examples, template literals, and checklist commands. They do not execute commands as part of the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown formatting or TypeScript examples, not executable skill code. No script file, hook, or command runner exists in the reported file.

low_findings.title: Benign Network Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch-related hits are illustrative TypeScript snippets about avoiding any and running async work in parallel. No external endpoint, credential transfer, or automatic request is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are generic placeholder examples without URLs, secrets, or execution flow. The skill file itself cannot initiate those requests.

low_findings.title: Benign Filesystem Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem hits appear in examples for streaming large files and parsing local configuration. They are legitimate Node.js teaching examples and do not read marketplace host files automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are inside Markdown TypeScript examples with placeholder paths. There is no runtime wrapper, hidden script, or instruction to access sensitive files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Flags Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, SQLite, reconnaissance, and network scanning detections match ordinary words or code examples such as description fields, SQL query text, process usage, and type literals. No crypto implementation, scanner invocation, or sensitive database access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows instructional code and text, not cryptographic routines or system probing. The detections appear substring-based.

content.value_statement: TypeScript and Node.js projects can become hard to maintain when typing, errors, tests, and module boundaries drift. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a strict engineering checklist for safer implementation and review.
actual_capabilities: Defines strict tsconfig settings for modern TypeScript projects.
Guides replacement of any types with unknown, generics, and type guards.
Recommends async, streaming, and generator patterns for performance-sensitive code.
Provides patterns for custom errors, Result types, and expected failure handling.
Covers Commander.js CLI structure, logging, user feedback, and test setup.
Supplies a practical checklist for TypeScript and Node.js code review.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable tooling or automation.
Examples are generic and must be adapted to each repository architecture.
It does not validate dependencies, package versions, or build output by itself.
It focuses on TypeScript and Node.js, not browser UI design or non-JavaScript stacks.

use_cases.title: Review a Node.js Pull Request
use_cases.description: Apply strict typing, async, error handling, and test coverage checks before merging TypeScript changes.

use_cases.title: Build a Maintainable CLI
use_cases.description: Design Commander.js commands, options, user feedback, and failure handling for a Node.js command line tool.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy TypeScript
use_cases.description: Replace unsafe types, improve null handling, split modules, and add focused Vitest coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Type Safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one file or function
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript code for strict typing issues. Focus on any, null safety, return types, and simple fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Node.js Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Node.js TypeScript module to use clear custom errors or a Result pattern for expected failures.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production CLI
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a maintainable TypeScript CLI using Commander.js. Include command structure, typed options, logging, validation, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Performance and Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a larger codebase
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this TypeScript Node.js code for performance, streaming opportunities, dependency boundaries, async behavior, and test gaps.

output_examples.input: Review a TypeScript service that uses any and throws generic errors.
output: A prioritized list of type safety issues and concrete replacements.
A proposed error model with expected and unexpected failure paths separated.
Test cases for success, validation failure, and downstream service failure.

output_examples.input: Plan a new Node.js CLI for processing local files.
output: A command structure with typed arguments, options, and dry-run behavior.
Guidance for streaming large inputs without loading full files into memory.
A Vitest test plan for parser, command action, and error reporting behavior.

output_examples.input: Refactor a module with sequential async calls and weak tests.
output: Recommendations for safe parallel execution where dependencies allow it.
A module boundary plan that separates data access from business logic.
Coverage targets and missing edge cases to add before release.

best_practices: Start with strict TypeScript settings before adding new project code.
Use type guards, Result objects, and custom errors to make failure paths explicit.
Run lint, typecheck, and tests after changing TypeScript or Node.js behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not use any to bypass modeling unknown input or external responses.
Do not load large files fully into memory when streaming is practical.
Do not mix CLI argument parsing, business logic, and filesystem writes in one function.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or install packages?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill with examples and review criteria.

faq.question: Can it help with existing TypeScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide reviews, refactors, test planning, and stricter configuration.

faq.question: Does it support both libraries and CLI tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers module organization, CLI structure, testing, errors, and performance.

faq.question: Is it suitable for beginner developers?
faq.answer: Yes, but some patterns assume basic TypeScript and Node.js knowledge.

faq.question: Does it replace a real security audit?
faq.answer: No. It improves code quality but does not perform dependency or runtime security scanning.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
